-hwaccel:
Hardware-accelerated decoding (input side).

Offloads decoding from the CPU to the GPU, reducing processor load — especially useful for 4K/8K content, long conversions, and batch processing.

VALUES:
  auto             FFmpeg automatically selects the best available method. Recommended in most cases.

  cuda             NVIDIA GPU (NVDEC / CUVID). Can reduce CPU load by 50–80% for high-resolution content. Requires: NVIDIA GPU + recent driver.

  videotoolbox     Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4) and Intel Mac hardware decoding. Best choice on macOS. Requires: macOS 10.13+, no extra installation.

  vaapi            Linux Intel/AMD hardware acceleration (VA-API). Requires: libva + appropriate GPU driver.

  qsv              Intel QuickSync Video. Available on Windows and Linux with Intel iGPU. Very fast.

  d3d11va          Windows Direct3D 11 hardware decoding. Usually `auto` is a better choice.

NOTE:
  This only accelerates DECODING. For hardware ENCODING, change -c:v: h264_videotoolbox, h264_nvenc, h264_qsv, etc.

  HW decode + SW encode: pixel format is converted automatically.
  HW → HW pipeline may require: -vf hwupload,scale_cuda=...
-ss:
Seek / trim start position.

CRITICAL: placing -ss before or after -i produces completely different behaviour!

-SS BEFORE -I (fast, less precise):
  FFmpeg jumps to the nearest I-frame (keyframe) — extremely fast but the cut may be off by several seconds. Ideal for -c copy (cutting without re-encoding).

  ffmpeg -ss 00:01:00 -i input.mp4 -c copy out.mp4

-SS AFTER -I (slow, frame-accurate):
  FFmpeg decodes from the start and discards frames until the given position. Slower but perfectly accurate. Use when re-encoding.

  ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -ss 00:01:00 -c:v libx264 out.mp4

FORMATS:
  HH:MM:SS            00:01:30        1 minute 30 seconds
  HH:MM:SS.mmm        00:01:30.500    millisecond precision
  seconds             90              90 seconds
  fractional seconds  90.5

COMBINING:
  -ss 00:01:00 -to 00:02:30   → from 1:00 to 2:30 (1 min 30 sec)
  -ss 00:01:00 -t 00:00:30    → from 1:00 for 30 seconds
-to:
Absolute trim end point.

Used together with -ss to extract a segment. The timestamp is relative to the beginning of the file (not relative to -ss).

FORMATS:
  HH:MM:SS            00:02:30        2 minutes 30 seconds
  seconds             150

EXAMPLES:
  ffmpeg -ss 00:01:00 -to 00:02:30 -i in.mp4 -c copy out.mp4
    → extract from 1:00 to 2:30 (fast, rounds to keyframe)

  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -ss 00:01:00 -to 00:02:30 -c:v libx264 out.mp4
    → frame-accurate segment with re-encoding

-TO VS -T:
  -to 00:02:30   → output stops at position 2:30 in the file
  -t  00:01:30   → output is 1 minute 30 seconds long
  (if -ss 00:01:00, both give the same result)
-t:
Output duration (alternative to -to).

Extracts a segment of the given length from the source, starting from the position set by -ss.

FORMATS:
  seconds      30           30 seconds
  HH:MM:SS     00:00:30

EXAMPLES:
  ffmpeg -ss 00:01:00 -t 30 -i in.mp4 -c copy out.mp4
    → 30 seconds starting from 1:00 (fast cut)

  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -ss 00:01:00 -t 00:00:30 -c:v libx264 out.mp4
    → frame-accurate 30-second segment

-T VS -TO:
  -t 30          → generate 30 seconds of output
  -to 00:01:30   → stop at position 1:30 in the file
  (if -ss 00:01:00, the above -to 00:01:30 and -t 30 are equivalent)
-c:v:
Video encoder — the most important setting.

Determines the re-encoding algorithm and controls output quality, file size, compatibility, and encoding speed.

VALUES:
  copy          No re-encoding. The video bitstream is copied unchanged. Fast and lossless, but resolution, quality, and CRF cannot be changed. Ideal for cutting or container conversion.

  libx264       H.264 / AVC — the most widely supported codec. Plays on virtually every device and platform. Recommended CRF: 18–28 (default 23). Ideal for web, archiving, and general use.

  libx265       H.265 / HEVC — ~40% smaller files at the same quality as H.264. Slower encoding. Recommended CRF: 24–28 (default 28). Ideal for 4K content, archiving, storage efficiency.

  libvpx-vp9    VP9 — Google's open codec (WebM container). Good quality/size ratio, natively supported by browsers. Ideal for YouTube uploads, web video.

  libaom-av1    AV1 — the most modern open codec. Best compression, but software encoding is very slow (10–50× libx264). Ideal for archiving where size matters more than speed.

  prores_ks     Apple ProRes — broadcast/editing intra-frame format. Profiles: 0=proxy, 1=lt, 2=standard, 3=hq, 4=4444. Ideal for Final Cut Pro, DaVinci Resolve projects.

  dnxhd         Avid DNxHD / DNxHR — Avid broadcast editing codec. Natively supported by DaVinci Resolve and Avid Media Composer.

  ffv1          FFV1 — lossless, open video archive format. Use with MKV container. Compressed but lossless. Ideal for long-term storage of digitised archival material.

HARDWARE ENCODERS (faster, but lower quality at the same settings):
  h264_videotoolbox   macOS Apple Silicon / Intel
  h264_nvenc          NVIDIA GPU (Windows/Linux)
  h264_qsv            Intel QuickSync
  hevc_videotoolbox   macOS HEVC hardware

EXAMPLES:
  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -c:v libx264 -crf 23 -preset slow out.mp4
  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -c:v libx265 -crf 28 -preset medium out.mp4
  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -c:v copy -c:a aac out.mp4
-crf:
Constant Rate Factor — quality-based variable bitrate encoding.

CRF optimises for human visual perception: fewer bits for motion, more for static areas. Output file size cannot be predicted, but quality remains consistent throughout.

RANGE:
  0          Lossless (huge file, only for special cases)
  15–17      Visually near-lossless — for editing masters
  18–22      Excellent quality — nearly indistinguishable from source
  23         Default (libx264) — good balance
  24–26      Good quality — suitable for web/streaming
  27–30      Acceptable — small file size, visible degradation
  35–51      Low quality — previews or heavy compression

DEFAULT BY CODEC:
  libx264     default CRF = 23
  libx265     default CRF = 28 (same visual quality, smaller file)
  libvpx-vp9  use -b:v 0 -crf 33 combination

NOTE:
  CRF and -b:v (bitrate) are mutually exclusive — if -b:v is set, it overrides CRF. Choose one.

EXAMPLES:
  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -c:v libx264 -crf 18 -preset slow out.mp4
  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -c:v libx265 -crf 26 -preset medium out.mp4
-preset:
Encoding speed vs. compression efficiency trade-off.

Slower presets produce smaller files at the same quality by using more advanced analysis. Encoding time increases significantly, but quality for a given file size improves.

PRESETS (slowest to fastest):
  veryslow   Best compression — 4–8× slower than medium
  slower     Very good compression — 2–3× slower
  slow       Good compression — recommended for archiving
  medium     Default — good balance of speed and quality
  fast       Slightly larger files, noticeably faster
  faster     Larger files, much faster
  veryfast   Large files, very fast — suitable for quick previews
  superfast  Minimal compression, near-realtime speed
  ultrafast  No compression optimization — only for live streaming

GUIDELINES:
  • For archiving: slow or veryslow
  • For daily use: medium
  • For previews / quick checks: fast or veryfast
  • For live encoding: superfast or ultrafast

NOTE: Preset names vary by hardware encoder. NVENC uses: slow, medium, fast, hp, hq, bd, ll, llhq, llhp, lossless.
-b:v:
Video bitrate — fixed/target bitrate encoding.

The encoder targets this bitrate for the entire file. File size is predictable, but quality varies by scene complexity.

FORMATS:
  k = kilobits/s    2000k = 2 Mbit/s
  M = megabits/s    2M    = 2 Mbit/s

TYPICAL VALUES:
  480p web         800k – 1500k
  720p streaming   2000k – 4000k
  1080p web        4000k – 8000k
  4K streaming     15M – 25M
  ProRes / DNxHD   36M – 185M

NOTE:
  -b:v and -crf are mutually exclusive. -b:v overrides CRF.
  For best quality use CRF; use -b:v only when a specific file size is required (e.g. platform upload limits).
-maxrate:
Maximum allowed video bitrate.

Used together with -bufsize for VBV (Video Buffer Verifier) rate control — ensures the bitrate never exceeds a defined ceiling. Required for streaming and hardware decoder compatibility.

FORMATS:
  k = kilobits/s    8000k
  M = megabits/s    8M

TYPICAL USE:
  -b:v 4M -maxrate 6M -bufsize 8M   → target 4 Mbit/s, max 6 Mbit/s

NOTE: Without -b:v, -maxrate has no effect (the encoder needs a target rate to cap).
-vf:
Video filter chain.

Applies one or more video filters in sequence. Multiple filters are separated by commas.

COMMON FILTERS:
  scale=1920:1080           Resize to 1920×1080
  scale=1280:-1             Width 1280px, height auto (keep aspect ratio)
  scale=1280:-2             Same, but height rounded to even number
  crop=w:h:x:y              Crop to w×h starting at (x, y)
  pad=1920:1080:(ow-iw)/2:(oh-ih)/2   Letterbox/pillarbox to 1920×1080
  transpose=1               Rotate 90° clockwise
  hflip / vflip             Horizontal / vertical flip
  fps=30                    Change frame rate to 30 fps
  setsar=1                  Set sample aspect ratio to 1:1
  format=yuv420p            Convert pixel format
  colorspace=bt709          Convert colour space
  drawtext=...              Overlay text

EXAMPLES:
  -vf scale=1280:720
  -vf scale=1920:1080,format=yuv420p
  -vf "scale=1280:-2,fps=30,format=yuv420p"
-r:
Frame rate (fps) of the output video.

Sets the number of frames per second in the output. FFmpeg will drop or duplicate frames as needed to reach the target rate.

TYPICAL VALUES:
  23.976    Film (NTSC-compatible)
  24        Cinema
  25        PAL / European broadcast
  29.97     NTSC broadcast
  30        Standard HD
  50        European HD broadcast
  59.94     NTSC HD broadcast
  60        High-frame-rate
  120       High-speed / slow-motion

NOTE:
  For fractional rates use: -r 24000/1001 (= 23.976)
  -r applied to input reads the source at that rate (rarely needed).
  To change fps without changing speed, use -vf fps=30 instead.
  Avoid setting -r unless necessary — mismatched rates cause frame duplication.
-pix_fmt:
Pixel format — colour depth and chroma subsampling.

Defines how colour information is stored per pixel. Affects compatibility, quality, and file size.

COMMON VALUES:
  yuv420p     4:2:0 — most compatible. Required by most web players, mobile devices, and H.264 Baseline/Main profile. Default for -c:v libx264.
  yuv422p     4:2:2 — better chroma accuracy. Used in broadcast and editing (ProRes, DNxHD).
  yuv444p     4:4:4 — full chroma. Used in lossless encoding, some ProRes profiles.
  yuv420p10le 10-bit 4:2:0 — HDR content (HDR10, Dolby Vision, HLG). For HEVC/AV1.
  yuv422p10le 10-bit 4:2:2 — high-quality broadcast, ProRes 4444.
  gbrp        RGB planar — for lossless workflows.
  rgba        32-bit RGBA — with alpha channel.

NOTE: Always set -pix_fmt yuv420p for H.264 output intended for web/mobile playback.
-profile:v:
Video codec profile — feature set and compatibility level.

H.264 PROFILES (from most to least compatible):
  baseline    Maximum compatibility (mobile, old devices). No B-frames, no CABAC.
  main        Standard compatibility. Includes B-frames and CABAC entropy coding.
  high        Default for HD/4K. Best quality/size ratio. Used for Blu-ray.
  high10      10-bit colour depth support.
  high422     10-bit + 4:2:2 chroma subsampling.
  high444     10-bit + 4:4:4 + lossless encoding.

H.265 PROFILES:
  main        8-bit, 4:2:0 — standard HDR and SDR.
  main10      10-bit, 4:2:0 — HDR10.
  main422-10  10-bit, 4:2:2.
  main444-10  10-bit, 4:4:4.

ProRes PROFILES (-profile:v value):
  0 = Proxy, 1 = LT, 2 = Standard, 3 = HQ, 4 = 4444, 5 = 4444XQ

NOTE: Profile must be compatible with the pixel format. For web H.264 use: -profile:v high -pix_fmt yuv420p
-refs:
Number of reference frames for H.264/H.265.

The encoder can reference this many previous frames when compressing the current frame. More references = better compression but slower encoding and higher decoder requirements.

RANGE: 1–16
  1–3     Fast decoding, basic devices (set-top boxes, older mobiles)
  4–8     Good balance of quality and compatibility
  16      Maximum compression — high-end decoders only

NOTE:
  H.264 Level 4.1 (Blu-ray) allows a maximum of 4 reference frames at 1080p.
  For web delivery set -refs 4 or leave at default.
  More refs greatly increase encoder memory usage.
-bf:
Maximum number of consecutive B-frames.

B-frames (bidirectional frames) reference both past and future frames. They significantly improve compression but increase decode complexity.

RANGE: 0–16
  0       No B-frames — maximum compatibility (H.264 Baseline)
  2–3     Default — good compression with standard compatibility
  8–16    Maximum compression, slow encoding, high decoder load

NOTE:
  H.264 Baseline profile does not support B-frames (-bf 0 required).
  For streaming targets (Apple HLS, DASH) 2–3 B-frames are recommended.
  B-frames require more encoder and decoder memory.
-tune:
Encoder tuning for specific content type.

Adjusts encoder parameters to optimise for a particular type of source material.

H.264 / H.265 TUNES:
  film        Cinematic content with natural grain. Better detail preservation.
  animation   Animated content with solid colours and sharp edges.
  grain       Preserves film grain — avoids smoothing noise as part of the image.
  stillimage  Encoding of still images or slideshows.
  fastdecode  Optimises for fast decoding on limited hardware (fewer B-frames, simpler deblocking).
  zerolatency Minimal latency — for live streaming and real-time applications. Disables B-frames and lookahead.
  psnr        Optimises for PSNR metric — primarily for quality benchmarking.
  ssim        Optimises for SSIM metric — primarily for quality benchmarking.

NOTE: Leave empty if none of the above fits — the default is usually optimal for general content.
-g:
GOP size — keyframe interval.

Sets how often an I-frame (keyframe) is inserted. Every GOP (Group of Pictures) starts with an I-frame.

EFFECT:
  Smaller GOP (e.g. -g 25):   Faster seeking, easier editing, larger files.
  Larger GOP (e.g. -g 250):   Better compression, smaller files, slower seeking.

TYPICAL VALUES:
  25    PAL broadcast (1× fps)
  30    NTSC broadcast
  48    2× 24fps (common streaming default)
  250   Long GOP — streaming platforms (YouTube, Netflix)

FORCED KEYFRAMES:
  Use -force_key_frames "expr:gte(t,n_forced*2)" to insert keyframes every 2 seconds regardless of fps.
-bufsize:
VBV buffer size — rate control buffer.

Defines the size of the decoder's hypothetical receive buffer. Used together with -maxrate to enforce a maximum bitrate ceiling.

FORMATS:
  k = kilobits    8000k
  M = megabits    8M

GUIDELINE:
  bufsize = 1.5–2× maxrate  (e.g. -maxrate 8M -bufsize 16M)

USE CASES:
  Streaming platforms typically specify required values (e.g. YouTube: -maxrate 8M -bufsize 8M for 1080p).
-vn:
Disable video stream.

Excludes all video from the output — only audio (and other streams) are written.

USE CASES:
  • Extract audio track from a video file.
  • Create audio-only output (MP3, WAV, FLAC, AAC, etc.).

EXAMPLE:
  ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -vn -c:a copy audio.m4a
  ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -vn -c:a libmp3lame -b:a 192k output.mp3
-c:a:
Audio encoder.

Determines the audio codec used for the output.

VALUES:
  copy          No re-encoding. Audio stream copied unchanged. Fast and lossless.
  aac           AAC — default for MP4/MOV. Widely compatible.
  libmp3lame    MP3 — universal compatibility.
  libopus       Opus — best quality at low bitrates. Ideal for VoIP and web streaming.
  libvorbis     Vorbis — open codec, OGG container.
  flac          FLAC — lossless, open format.
  pcm_s16le     PCM 16-bit signed little-endian — uncompressed WAV.
  pcm_s24le     PCM 24-bit — studio/DAW quality.
  pcm_s32le     PCM 32-bit — professional archiving.
  eac3          Dolby Digital Plus (E-AC-3).
  ac3           Dolby Digital (AC-3) — DVD/Blu-ray.
  dts           DTS — Blu-ray, home cinema.
  alac          Apple Lossless — lossless, MOV/M4A container.
  truehd        Dolby TrueHD — Blu-ray.
  pcm_f32le     32-bit float PCM — professional use.

NOTE: For DAW export: pcm_s24le is recommended for 24-bit WAV.
-b:a:
Audio bitrate.

Sets the target bitrate for the audio encoder.

FORMATS:
  k = kilobits/s    192k

TYPICAL VALUES:
  MP3 / AAC:
    96k     Acceptable (voice, podcasts)
    128k    Good (general streaming)
    192k    Very good (music)
    256k    Excellent
    320k    Maximum MP3 quality

  Opus:
    48k     Excellent voice quality
    64k–96k High-quality music
    128k    Near-transparent

  For lossless codecs (FLAC, PCM): -b:a has no effect.
-ar:
Audio sample rate (Hz).

Sets the number of audio samples per second in the output.

COMMON VALUES:
  8000     Telephone quality
  22050    Low quality audio
  44100    CD quality — standard for music
  48000    Professional standard — required for video/broadcast
  88200    High-resolution (2× CD)
  96000    High-resolution — studio/archiving
  192000   Ultra-high-resolution

NOTE:
  Video files should always use 48000 Hz.
  Mismatched sample rates between codec and container can cause A/V sync issues.
  Converting from 44100 to 48000 (or vice versa) requires resampling.
-ac:
Number of audio channels.

Sets the channel count in the output.

VALUES:
  1    Mono
  2    Stereo (most common)
  4    Quadraphonic (rarely used)
  6    5.1 surround
  8    7.1 surround

NOTE:
  Downmixing (e.g. 5.1 → stereo) is automatic but may not be optimal — use -af "pan=..." or loudnorm for better control.
  Upmixing (mono → stereo) simply duplicates the channel.
  AAC supports maximum 8 channels; MP3 supports maximum 2 channels.
-af:
Audio filter chain.

Applies one or more audio filters in sequence. Multiple filters are separated by commas.

COMMON FILTERS:
  volume=2.0             Amplify by 2× (+6 dB)
  volume=-6dB            Attenuate by 6 dB
  loudnorm               EBU R128 loudness normalization (broadcast standard)
  loudnorm=I=-16:TP=-1.5:LRA=11   Normalize to -16 LUFS, -1.5 dBTP
  aresample=48000        Resample to 48 kHz
  pan=stereo|c0=c2|c1=c3          Route channels (e.g. extract centre pair from 5.1)
  equalizer=f=1000:t=o:w=200:g=3  EQ boost at 1 kHz
  compand                Dynamic range compression
  highpass=f=80          High-pass filter at 80 Hz
  lowpass=f=16000        Low-pass filter at 16 kHz
  adelay=500|500         Add 500 ms delay to both channels
  silenceremove          Remove silence at start/end
  channelmap=0|0         Duplicate mono to both stereo channels

EXAMPLES:
  -af "loudnorm=I=-16:TP=-1.5:LRA=11"
  -af "volume=1.5,aresample=48000"
-an:
Disable audio stream.

Excludes all audio from the output — only video (and other streams) are written.

USE CASES:
  • Create video-only output (mute the file).
  • Add a different audio track later.

EXAMPLE:
  ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -an -c:v copy video_only.mp4
-vol:
Audio volume adjustment (integer, 256 = 100%).

Adjusts playback volume by multiplying the audio sample values. 256 = original volume.

VALUES:
  128    50% volume (-6 dB)
  256    Original (100%)
  512    200% volume (+6 dB)
  0      Silent

NOTE:
  This is a legacy option. For most use cases, use -af volume= instead:
    -af "volume=2.0"     → 200%
    -af "volume=-6dB"    → attenuate by 6 dB
  Clipping may occur if volume exceeds the digital ceiling.
-map:
Explicit stream selection.

By default FFmpeg selects one video and one audio stream automatically. -map overrides this.

SYNTAX:
  -map 0          All streams from input 0
  -map 0:v        All video streams from input 0
  -map 0:a        All audio streams from input 0
  -map 0:a:0      First audio stream from input 0
  -map 0:a:1      Second audio stream from input 0
  -map 0:s        All subtitle streams
  -map -0:a:0     Exclude first audio stream
  -map 1:a:0      First audio stream from second input file

EXAMPLES:
  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -map 0:v -map 0:a:0 out.mp4
    → video + first audio only

  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -map 0 out.mkv
    → all streams (video, audio, subtitles, attachments)

  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -i audio.wav -map 0:v -map 1:a out.mp4
    → video from file 1, audio from file 2
-movflags:
MP4/MOV container flags.

Controls special container-level options for MP4 and MOV output.

VALUES:
  faststart           Moves the moov atom to the beginning of the file. Required for HTTP progressive download and web streaming. Essential for web use.
  frag_keyframe       Fragment the file at every keyframe. Required for DASH streaming.
  empty_moov          Write an empty moov atom before the media data. Enables live/incomplete-file playback.
  +write_colr         Write colour information metadata.
  negative_cts_offsets  Enable negative CTS offsets (required for some H.264 presets).

NOTE:
  -movflags faststart is the most commonly needed option — always set it for web delivery.
-metadata:
Set output file metadata.

Adds or overwrites metadata tags in the output container.

SYNTAX:
  -metadata key=value

COMMON KEYS:
  title         Title of the content
  artist        Artist / creator
  album         Album or collection name
  comment       Comment / description
  year          Year of creation
  track         Track number
  genre         Genre
  language      Language code (e.g. eng, hun)
  copyright     Copyright notice
  encoder       Encoder software name

EXAMPLES:
  -metadata title="My Video" -metadata artist="John Doe"
  -metadata:s:a:0 language=eng   → set language on first audio stream
-threads:
Number of CPU threads used for encoding/decoding.

Sets the thread count available to the codec.

VALUES:
  0       Auto (FFmpeg detects core count) — default, recommended
  1       Single-threaded — for debugging or deterministic output
  N       Use exactly N threads

NOTE:
  Most codecs (libx264, libx265, libopus) manage threading internally and ignore this setting.
  The option primarily affects codecs with native FFmpeg threading (e.g. mjpeg, mpeg2video, ffv1).
  Generally leave at 0.
-loglevel:
FFmpeg log verbosity.

Controls how much diagnostic output is printed to stderr.

LEVELS (quietest to noisiest):
  quiet      No output (not even errors)
  panic      Only fatal errors that abort processing
  fatal      Fatal errors only
  error      All errors (default threshold for most builds)
  warning    Errors + warnings
  info       Normal informational output (FFmpeg default)
  verbose    Detailed info including per-frame data
  debug      Full debug output (very large output)
  trace      Maximum verbosity (rarely needed)

FLAGS:
  -loglevel repeat      Show repeated log lines (default: suppressed)
  -loglevel level+flags Allow combining flags

USE CASES:
  -loglevel error     Suppress progress output in batch scripts
  -loglevel quiet     Fully silent mode
-y:
Overwrite output file without asking.

If the output file already exists, FFmpeg will overwrite it automatically without a confirmation prompt.

DEFAULT BEHAVIOUR:
  Without -y: FFmpeg asks "Overwrite? (y/n)", which blocks automation.
  With -y: Silent overwrite — file is replaced immediately.

NOTE:
  Use with care in scripts. There is no undo. The opposite flag is -n (never overwrite, always skip).
-f:
Force input or output format.

Forces FFmpeg to use a specific container format or demuxer, overriding automatic detection.

USE CASES:
  Input (overriding auto-detect):
    -f mpegts -i /dev/video0    → force MPEG-TS demuxer
    -f rawvideo -i pipe:0       → read raw video from stdin

  Output (force container/muxer):
    -f mp4    → force MP4 muxer (useful when output has no extension)
    -f null   → discard output (used for benchmarking / analysis)
    -f rawvideo -pix_fmt yuv420p pipe:1  → raw video to stdout

COMMON FORMAT NAMES:
  mp4, mkv, mov, avi, mpegts, hls, dash, flac, mp3, wav, ogg, image2, rawvideo, null

NOTE: Usually not needed — FFmpeg detects format from file extension.
-c:s:
Subtitle codec.

Sets the codec for subtitle streams.

VALUES:
  copy      Copy subtitle stream unchanged (most common)
  mov_text  MP4 subtitle format (TEXT → MOV_TEXT, required for MP4)
  srt       SRT text subtitles
  ass       ASS/SSA advanced subtitles
  webvtt    WebVTT — web standard
  dvd_subtitle  DVD bitmap subtitles (VobSub)
  hdmv_pgs_subtitle  Blu-ray PGS bitmap subtitles

NOTE:
  When converting MKV (ASS) → MP4, use -c:s mov_text.
  Bitmap subtitles (PGS, VobSub) cannot be converted to text subtitles automatically.
-sn:
Disable subtitle streams.

Excludes all subtitle streams from the output.

USE CASE:
  When you want a clean output without embedded subtitles.

EXAMPLE:
  ffmpeg -i input.mkv -sn -c:v copy -c:a copy output.mp4
-map_metadata:
Metadata mapping between streams.

Controls how metadata is copied from the input to the output.

VALUES:
  0         Copy all metadata from first input (default)
  -1        Remove all metadata from output (clean file)
  0:g       Copy global metadata from input 0
  0:s:0     Copy metadata from stream 0 of input 0

USE CASES:
  -map_metadata -1    → strip all metadata (privacy, clean export)
  -map_metadata 0     → preserve all metadata (default)
-fflags:
FFmpeg format/demuxer flags.

Controls low-level format-level behaviour.

COMMON VALUES:
  +genpts       Generate missing presentation timestamps. Useful for streams with broken timestamps.
  +igndts       Ignore decode timestamps — use only presentation timestamps.
  +discardcorrupt  Discard corrupt packets instead of failing.
  +sortdts      Sort packets by DTS.
  nobuffer      Reduce input buffering for low-latency capture.
  +fastseek     Enable fast but less accurate seeking.

USE CASES:
  -fflags +genpts    → fix MPEG-TS streams or capture cards with missing timestamps
  -fflags nobuffer   → low-latency capture (webcam, screen capture)
-avoid_negative_ts:
Handling of negative timestamps.

Some containers or sources produce negative timestamps, which can cause playback issues in players.

VALUES:
  make_non_negative   Shift timestamps so the first one is 0 (most common fix)
  make_zero           Force the first timestamp to exactly 0
  disabled            Leave timestamps unchanged
  auto                FFmpeg decides based on the muxer (default)

NOTE: Usually needed when cutting from the middle of a stream (-ss) and output timestamps become negative.
-probesize:
Amount of data read for format/stream detection.

FFmpeg reads this many bytes from the input to detect the format, codec, and stream parameters.

FORMAT:
  bytes (integer)    1000000 = 1 MB

DEFAULT: 5 MB

USE CASES:
  Increase: when FFmpeg fails to detect streams in files with late headers (e.g. some AVI, TS)
    -probesize 50M
  Decrease: for fast detection of simple streams or when startup latency matters
    -probesize 1M

NOTE: Use together with -analyzeduration for best results.
-re:
Read input at native frame rate.

Reads the input at the rate defined by its timestamps (real-time speed), instead of as fast as possible.

USE CASES:
  • Simulate live streaming from a file.
  • Stream a file to an RTMP/SRT server at correct speed.
  • Avoid overwhelming a streaming server with too-fast input.

EXAMPLE:
  ffmpeg -re -i input.mp4 -c copy -f flv rtmp://server/live/stream
-stream_loop:
Loop the input stream.

Sets how many times the input is looped.

VALUES:
  0     No looping (play once) — default
  -1    Infinite loop
  N     Loop N times (total plays = N+1)

EXAMPLES:
  ffmpeg -stream_loop -1 -i input.mp4 -c copy output.mp4
    → infinite loop (terminates when output limit is reached, e.g. -t)

  ffmpeg -stream_loop 4 -i intro.mp4 -c copy output.mp4
    → play 5 times total
-itsoffset:
Input timestamp offset.

Shifts the timestamps of all streams from an input by the given value. Useful for A/V sync correction.

FORMAT:
  seconds (float)   0.5 = 500 ms offset
  HH:MM:SS          negative values allowed

EXAMPLES:
  ffmpeg -itsoffset 0.5 -i video.mp4 -i audio.wav -map 0:v -map 1:a out.mp4
    → delays the video by 500 ms relative to audio

  ffmpeg -itsoffset -0.3 -i audio.wav -i video.mp4 -map 1:v -map 0:a out.mp4
    → advances audio by 300 ms
-s:
Output video resolution (width×height).

Resizes the output to the specified dimensions. Equivalent to -vf scale=W:H.

FORMAT:
  WxH   1920x1080, 1280x720, 640x480

SHORTHANDS:
  hd480   640×480
  hd720   1280×720
  hd1080  1920×1080

NOTE: Prefer -vf scale= for more control (e.g. keep aspect ratio with -1 or -2).
-aspect:
Output video aspect ratio.

Sets the display aspect ratio (DAR) metadata in the output, without resizing the frame.

FORMAT:
  W:H   16:9, 4:3, 2.35:1

NOTE:
  -aspect does NOT resize the video — it only sets the metadata tag.
  For actual resizing, use -vf scale= or -s.
  Used when the stored pixel aspect ratio differs from the intended display ratio.
-vframes:
Number of video frames to output.

Limits the output to the specified number of video frames, then stops.

EXAMPLES:
  -vframes 1    → extract a single frame (thumbnail)
  -vframes 150  → extract 150 frames

COMMON USE: Extract a thumbnail
  ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -ss 00:00:05 -vframes 1 thumbnail.jpg

NOTE: Equivalent to -frames:v N
-level:
Codec level — decoder capability constraint.

H.264 LEVELS (most common):
  3.0   720p@30fps — older mobile devices, set-top boxes
  3.1   720p@30fps with higher bitrate — Blu-ray, HDTV
  4.0   1080p@30fps — widely supported
  4.1   1080p@30fps — Blu-ray standard maximum
  4.2   1080p@60fps — modern devices
  5.0   4K@30fps
  5.1   4K@60fps — modern streaming
  5.2   4K@60fps with higher bitrate
  6.0+  8K and above

NOTE: Level limits maximum bitrate, resolution, and reference frames simultaneously.
  For broad compatibility: -level 4.1 -profile:v high
  For modern playback: -level 5.1
-minrate:
Minimum video bitrate.

Prevents the encoder from going below this bitrate, ensuring a minimum quality floor in low-complexity scenes.

FORMAT:
  k = kilobits/s, M = megabits/s

TYPICAL USE:
  -b:v 4M -minrate 2M -maxrate 6M -bufsize 8M

NOTE: Rarely needed for file encoding. Primarily used in streaming to avoid very low-quality gaps in static scenes.
-pass:
Two-pass encoding.

Two-pass encoding achieves more accurate bitrate control than single-pass VBR.

PASS 1 (-pass 1):
  Analyses the source and writes statistics to a log file. Output can be discarded (-f null /dev/null).

PASS 2 (-pass 2):
  Uses the statistics file to allocate bits optimally across the video.

EXAMPLE:
  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -c:v libx264 -b:v 4M -pass 1 -f null /dev/null
  ffmpeg -i in.mp4 -c:v libx264 -b:v 4M -pass 2 out.mp4

NOTE: Only meaningful with fixed bitrate (-b:v). Not compatible with CRF mode.
-qscale:v:
Global video quality scale (VBR).

Sets a global quality factor for variable bitrate encoding with JPEG, MPEG-2, and MJPEG encoders.

RANGE: 1 (best) → 31 (worst)
  1–3     Near-lossless, large files
  3–7     Good quality
  8–12    Acceptable quality, smaller files

NOTE: Not applicable to H.264/H.265 — use -crf instead. For MJPEG: -qscale:v 2 is near-lossless.
-bsf:v:
Video bitstream filter.

Applies a low-level bitstream transformation to the encoded video data.

COMMON FILTERS:
  h264_mp4toannexb     Convert H.264 from MP4 (AVCC) to Annex B format. Required when putting H.264 into MPEG-TS or raw H.264 streams.
  hevc_mp4toannexb     Same for H.265/HEVC.
  dump_extra           Add extra data to every keyframe packet (for streaming).
  noise                Add noise for testing.
  remove_extradata     Remove codec extra data.

EXAMPLE:
  ffmpeg -i input.mp4 -c copy -bsf:v h264_mp4toannexb output.ts
-tag:v:
Video stream codec tag.

Sets a 4-character FourCC tag for the video stream in the container. Overrides the default tag chosen by the muxer.

COMMON USE CASES:
  -tag:v hvc1    Required for HEVC video in MP4/MOV containers for playback on Apple devices (iOS, macOS, QuickTime).
  -tag:v avc1    H.264 in MP4 (alternative to default mp4v).
  -tag:v dvh1    Dolby Vision HEVC.

NOTE: Normally not needed. Use -tag:v hvc1 when HEVC video is not recognised by Apple devices.
-vsync:
Video sync method.

Controls how FFmpeg synchronises video frame timestamps.

VALUES:
  0 / passthrough   Timestamps passed through unchanged.
  1 / cfr           Constant frame rate — duplicate/drop frames to achieve exact fps.
  2 / vfr           Variable frame rate — preserve source timestamps, no duplicate frames.
  -1 / auto         FFmpeg chooses automatically based on muxer (default).
  drop              Drop all timestamps — only valid for raw output.

NOTE: In modern FFmpeg (5.x+), use -fps_mode instead:
  -fps_mode passthrough / cfr / vfr / auto
-profile:a:
Audio codec profile.

Sets the encoding profile for the audio codec.

AAC PROFILES:
  aac_low    AAC-LC (Low Complexity) — most compatible, default
  aac_he     HE-AAC (High Efficiency / SBR) — better at low bitrates (≤ 64k)
  aac_he_v2  HE-AACv2 (Parametric Stereo) — stereo at 24–48k, best compression
  aac_ld     AAC-LD (Low Delay) — for real-time/broadcast
  aac_eld    AAC-ELD (Enhanced Low Delay) — VoIP applications

NOTE: HE-AACv2 (-profile:a aac_he_v2) is only effective at very low bitrates (< 48k) for stereo content.
-q:a:
Audio quality scale (VBR).

Sets the variable bitrate quality for audio codecs that support VBR encoding.

MP3 (libmp3lame):
  0     Best quality (~245 kbps)
  2     High quality (~190 kbps) — recommended
  4     Good quality (~165 kbps)
  6     Acceptable (~130 kbps)
  9     Lowest quality (~65 kbps)

Vorbis (libvorbis):
  Range: -1 to 10. Higher = better.
  3 ≈ 112 kbps, 6 ≈ 192 kbps

NOTE: Lower number = better quality for MP3; higher number = better quality for Vorbis.
-sample_fmt:
Audio sample format (bit depth).

Sets the sample format for the audio output.

COMMON VALUES:
  u8          8-bit unsigned
  s16         16-bit signed — CD quality, standard for most use
  s32         32-bit signed
  fltp        32-bit float, planar — internal FFmpeg default
  s16p        16-bit signed, planar
  s32p        32-bit signed, planar
  dblp        64-bit double, planar

NOTE: For WAV output, most players expect s16 or s32. fltp is FFmpeg's internal format and may not be directly playable.
-bsf:a:
Audio bitstream filter.

Applies a low-level transformation to the encoded audio data.

COMMON FILTERS:
  aac_adtstoasc    Convert AAC from ADTS format (TS streams) to MPEG-4 Audio Specific Config (MP4). Required when extracting AAC from MPEG-TS into MP4.
  mp3decomp        Decompose MP3 stream.
  remove_extradata Remove extra data from packets.

EXAMPLE:
  ffmpeg -i input.ts -c copy -bsf:a aac_adtstoasc output.mp4
-shortest:
End output when the shortest stream ends.

By default, FFmpeg waits for all streams to finish. -shortest stops encoding when any stream reaches its end.

USE CASES:
  • Combine a video with a shorter audio track — stop when audio ends.
  • Combine a static image with a music file — stop when music ends.

EXAMPLE:
  ffmpeg -loop 1 -i image.png -i audio.mp3 -shortest -c:v libx264 output.mp4
-max_muxing_queue_size:
Maximum muxer packet queue size.

Sets the maximum number of packets that can be buffered in the muxer queue per stream.

DEFAULT: 128

PROBLEM: "Too many packets buffered for output stream" error.
This occurs when one stream (usually video) needs a large lookahead buffer while another (usually audio) runs ahead.

FIX:
  -max_muxing_queue_size 1024   → increase the buffer size

NOTE: A larger value increases memory usage. Use 512 or 1024 for most cases.
-copyts:
Copy input timestamps to output.

Preserves the original timestamps from the input instead of recalculating them from zero.

USE CASES:
  • Maintain original timestamps when remuxing (container conversion without re-encoding).
  • Keep segment timestamps correct for HLS/DASH output.

NOTE: Without -copyts, FFmpeg resets timestamps to start from 0 in the output.
-start_at_zero:
Adjust copied timestamps to start at zero.

Used together with -copyts. Shifts timestamps so the output starts at 0, while still preserving relative timing.

USE CASE:
  When using -ss (seek) with -copyts, the output timestamps would start at the seek position. -start_at_zero corrects this so they begin from 0.
-strict:
Strictness of standard compliance.

Controls how strictly FFmpeg enforces codec standard compliance.

VALUES:
  very     Very strict — reject any non-standard content
  strict   Standard compliance
  normal   Default behaviour
  unofficial  Allow unofficial extensions
  experimental  Allow experimental codecs and features

COMMON USE:
  -strict experimental   → enable experimental codecs (e.g. older AAC encoder, some HEVC features)
  -strict -2             → same as experimental (numeric form)

NOTE: In older FFmpeg versions, AAC encoding required -strict experimental. Modern FFmpeg no longer needs this.
-analyzeduration:
Duration of input data to analyse for stream detection.

Sets how many microseconds of input data FFmpeg reads to detect stream parameters.

FORMAT:
  microseconds (integer)   5000000 = 5 seconds

DEFAULT: 5 seconds

USE CASES:
  Increase: when streams have late audio or video (MPEG-TS, some live streams):
    -analyzeduration 10M
  Decrease: for faster startup in simple files:
    -analyzeduration 1M

NOTE: Use together with -probesize for complete stream detection control.
-tag:a:
Audio stream codec tag.

Sets the FourCC tag for the audio stream in the container.

COMMON USE CASES:
  -tag:a ec-3    For E-AC-3 (Dolby Digital Plus) in MP4 containers.
  -tag:a mp4a    Standard AAC tag in MP4.

NOTE: Normally not needed. Use when players fail to recognise an audio stream despite correct encoding.
-dn:
Disable data streams.

Excludes all data streams from the output (e.g. timecode tracks, GoPro telemetry, embedded GPS data).

USE CASE:
  Clean up output files by removing unwanted data tracks.
-color_range:
Video colour range (limited vs. full).

Specifies whether the luma/chroma values use full range (0–255) or limited/broadcast range (16–235 for 8-bit).

VALUES:
  tv / mpeg / limited   Broadcast range (16–235 for 8-bit, 64–940 for 10-bit). Standard for video.
  pc / jpeg / full      Full range (0–255). Used in computer graphics and some cameras.

NOTE:
  Wrong colour range setting causes washed-out (limited interpreted as full) or crushed blacks (full interpreted as limited) video.
  Most cameras record in limited range. Computer-generated content often uses full range.
-color_primaries:
Colour primaries — the colour gamut definition.

Specifies which set of primary colours defines the colour space of the video.

COMMON VALUES:
  bt709      Standard HD — Rec. 709. Default for HD video.
  bt2020     Ultra-wide gamut — used for HDR10 and HLG content.
  bt470bg    PAL standard (legacy).
  smpte170m  NTSC standard (legacy).
  smpte432   DCI-P3 — digital cinema.
  film       Generic film primaries.

NOTE: Must match the actual content. Mismatch causes colour shift.
-color_trc:
Transfer characteristics (gamma / EOTF).

Defines how numerical values in the video signal map to light output (the electro-optical transfer function).

COMMON VALUES:
  bt709         Standard HD gamma. Default for most SDR content.
  smpte2084     PQ (Perceptual Quantizer) — HDR10, Dolby Vision.
  arib-std-b67  HLG (Hybrid Log-Gamma) — broadcast HDR.
  gamma22       sRGB-like gamma (γ = 2.2).
  linear        Linear light (no gamma).
  bt470m        NTSC legacy.

NOTE: HDR content requires smpte2084 (PQ) or arib-std-b67 (HLG) plus matching -color_primaries bt2020.
-colorspace:
YCbCr colour matrix coefficients.

Specifies the mathematical matrix used to convert between RGB and YCbCr colour representations.

COMMON VALUES:
  bt709      Standard HD — Rec. 709. Default for all HD content.
  bt2020nc   HDR content — Rec. 2020 non-constant luminance. Standard for HDR10/HLG.
  bt470bg    PAL legacy.
  smpte170m  NTSC legacy (same coefficients as bt470bg).
  fcc        Older US broadcast standard.

NOTE: Always tag correctly — wrong matrix causes colour cast (typically greenish or purplish tones).
-chroma_sample_location:
Chroma sample location.

Specifies where chroma samples are positioned relative to the luma grid in 4:2:0 and 4:2:2 video.

VALUES:
  left        MPEG-2 standard — chroma co-sited with left luma sample. Standard for H.264/H.265 video.
  center      JPEG/DV standard — chroma centred between luma samples.
  topleft     Used in 4:2:0 4K video from some cameras.
  top         Rarely used.
  bottomleft  Rarely used.
  bottom      Rarely used.

NOTE: For most H.264/H.265 video: -chroma_sample_location left
-master_display:
HDR10 master display colour volume (SEI metadata).

Embeds HDR10 mastering display colour volume metadata into the bitstream. Required for compliant HDR10 delivery.

FORMAT:
  "G(x,y)B(x,y)R(x,y)WP(x,y)L(max,min)"
  Colour values are in 1/50000 units; luminance in 1/10000 nits.

EXAMPLE (P3-D65 mastered at 1000 nits):
  -master_display "G(13250,34500)B(7500,3000)R(34000,16000)WP(15635,16450)L(10000000,50)"

NOTE: Use together with -max_cll and -color_primaries bt2020 -color_trc smpte2084 for full HDR10 compliance.
-max_cll:
HDR10 maximum content light level (SEI metadata).

Embeds MaxCLL (Maximum Content Light Level) and MaxFALL (Maximum Frame Average Light Level) metadata into the bitstream.

FORMAT:
  "MaxCLL,MaxFALL"  (in nits)

EXAMPLE:
  -max_cll "1000,400"
    → MaxCLL = 1000 nits (brightest single pixel)
    → MaxFALL = 400 nits (brightest average frame)

NOTE: These values are usually provided by the mastering studio or measured from the source. Use together with -master_display for complete HDR10 metadata.
